sections.question: The California Democratic Party requests FPPC's advice on the disclosure requirements incurred by both the State Party and the County Central Committees when they combine in a joint statewide fundraising program. The name of the program is VIP (Volunteers in Politics). Basically the plan would involve solicitation of membership contributions from individuals to a statewide fundraising plan. The County Committees would solicit contributions to the program from within their counties, and all money contributed by individual members would be sent directly to a central State Committee account, with the County Committees acting as agents for the state account when necessary. Upon depositing contributions in the program's central State account, the State Committee will, every 90 days: redistribute 2/3 of the amount received from a county's member contributors back to that county's Committee. The State Committee will retain 1/3 for its own use. We would like to know what the reporting obligations are for the State Committee account and what the reporting obligations would be for the County Central Committees, particularly upon receiving their 2/3 share back from the State Committee account.
sections.conclusion: The State Committee will be deemed an intermediary for 2/3 of every dollar received from the counties and returned to the counties. This means that the counties will have the obligation of reporting all contributors from whom $50 or more is received by the VIP fund during a cumulative period. The county committee must disclose the state as an intermediary with respect to each such contribution. 1/3 of every dollar will be a direct contribution to the State Committee. The State Committee will have an obligation to report all contributors all persons who contribute $150 by this means since their share of the contribution would be 1/3.
sections.question_synthetic: What are the disclosure and reporting obligations for the State Committee and County Central Committees under a joint VIP fundraising program where county committees solicit contributions that flow through a central state account, with 2/3 redistributed back to counties and 1/3 retained by the state?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The State Committee serves as an intermediary for 2/3 of contributions flowing back to counties, requiring counties to report contributors of $50+ and disclose the state as intermediary. The remaining 1/3 becomes a direct state contribution, requiring the State Committee to report contributors of $150+.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request letter (August 19, 1977) and FPPC response letter (September 20, 1977). Response is from Regina Siciliano-Kutchins, Technical Assistance Program Coordinator. OCR quality is poor in places, affecting confidence level. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers present, but question and conclusion content clearly identified in letter body.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The California Democratic Party requests FPPC's advice on the disclosure requirements incurred by both the State Party and the County Central Committees when they combine in a joint statewide fundraising program. The name of the program is VIP (Volunteers in Politics). Basically the plan would involve solicitation of membership contributions from individuals to a statewide fundraising plan. The County Committees would solicit contributions to the program from within their counties, and all money contributed by individual members would be sent directly to a central State Committee account, with the County Committees acting as agents for the state account when necessary. Upon depositing contributions in the program's central State account, the State Committee will, every 90 days: redistribute 2/3 of the amount received from a county's member contributors back to that county's Committee. The State Committee will retain 1/3 for its own use. We would like to know what the reporting obligations are for the State Committee account and what the reporting obligations would be for the County Central Committees, particularly upon receiving their 2/3 share back from the State Committee account.

CONCLUSION: The State Committee will be deemed an intermediary for 2/3 of every dollar received from the counties and returned to the counties. This means that the counties will have the obligation of reporting all contributors from whom $50 or more is received by the VIP fund during a cumulative period. The county committee must disclose the state as an intermediary with respect to each such contribution. 1/3 of every dollar will be a direct contribution to the State Committee. The State Committee will have an obligation to report all contributors all persons who contribute $150 by this means since their share of the contribution would be 1/3.

embedding.summary: FPPC provides advice to the California Democratic Party regarding disclosure requirements for its VIP (Volunteers in Politics) statewide fundraising program involving state and county committee coordination. The agency clarifies the reporting obligations for both the state and county committees based on their respective shares of contributed funds.
